title: Projects
text: In fall of 2024, I began to teach self leadership yoga classes sponsored by the Marian K. Shaughnessy Nurse Leadership Academy (MKSNLA). These classes are meant to give students space in your day to improve your own physical wellness and consequently improve how you see yourself as a leader and changemaker in the community.

title: Projects
section: Yoga and Meditation Class with CWRU Sky Meditation
text:
Yoga and Meditation Class with CWRU Sky Meditation









In the fall of 2024, CWRU Yoga Club partnered with the SKY meditation club for the first time to offer the community a free one hour vinyasa class and meditation. SKY Meditation is a unique breath-based technique that uses cyclical, rhythmic patterns of breath to bring the mind and body effortlessly into meditation. This technique is a great way for anyone to deep their toes into meditation. I look forward to expanding my yoga teaching beyond vinyasa style classes to incorporate restorative breathing.

title: February Blog Post
text:
I took 431 during my senior year of undergrad, and I am happy to report that since then I have continued on as an official Clinical and Translational Science PhD student. I am deeply grateful to be able to continue building a research career during these uncertain times, and I do not take that opportunity lightly.
One key theme I have tried to instill in my scientific process is keeping things simple.
Jeff Leek frequently emphasizes that simple, readable code is not a shortcut, but rather a mark of rigor and respect for the people who will read, use, and maintain your work. He argues that clarity in code is foundational to reproducibility, collaboration, and scientific trust. As data and code sharing become increasingly important, simplicity helps minimize ambiguity and reduces the risk of misinterpretation. Keeping things simple also resonates with me because it requires knowing how to ask the right questions. This skill takes a great deal of refinement, but I am slowly developing it through my meta-analysis course and a systems biology elective adapted from the Standard Biodesign curriculum. In both code and scientific writing, especially as I begin drafting my first T-32 this semester, words matter! Just like clean code, each word needs to flow to the next so the reader can follow the logic behind the ideas. It has also taught me the importance of a great visualization. and results. Learning “R” has taught me to pay attention to details I often overlook A clear visualization and explaination like our “Harry and Sally” comparisions may seem rudimentary, but the are one of the most valuable lessons I have learned. I have learned taking the time to refine your code and research aims early in the scientific process. In the long term, this saves you and your editors time in the long run.
Here’s a picture of me and my Grandma (who is 82) after my white coat ceremony. I was elated to celebrate with her, and my parents.

title: February Blog Post
section: An Update: CWRU Community Yoga Classes
text:
An Update: CWRU Community Yoga Classes









Last time this page was updated I shared I teach free community yoga classes at the HEC each week. Since that intiative began in partnership with the nursing students we have expanded to include brief meditations in all classes here at Case Western Reserve University! Nursing classes from bachelor’s of nursing students all the way to doctoral nursing students take part in a brief guided meditation at the start of class. I am working with the school now to publish these findings on student reported benefits of feeling more focused in the classroom after a brief meditation, and the benefits of free community yoga classes.

title: Madaline Witort
text:
Github
  
  
    
     LinkedIn
  
  
    
     Email
  




Madaline Witort
First year Clinical and Translational Science PhD student. I graduated from CWRU with a Bachelor’s of Arts in Communication Science and Disorders and Cognitive Science from Case Western Reserve University as well! I teach yoga classes to the CWRU Yoga Club and greater Cleveland community.
As a dedicated research scientist, with over four years spent understanding brain injury and translational research, my career is defined by moving scientific discoveries across the research continuum to improve patient outcomes. I am adept at evidence synthesis to understand gaps in the current literature as well as using computational modeling to demonstrate these findings. Not only am I an aspiring research scientist, but I am a mentor. In my role as a mentor for the PubMed Healthcare Analytics Program here at CWRU I mentored a cohort of seven undergraduate students ranging from bio-medical engineering students, pre-health students and pre-law students. I aim to continue to ask research questions that have measurable patient outcomes. I am committed to improving the statistical quality of research and believe in the power of interdisciplinary research to solve health challenges.
On this website, you will find a selection of my published works, including scholarly articles, as well as information on upcoming events and conferences where I will be speaking. I invite you to explore my website. Please feel free to contact me if you have any questions or would like to discuss potential projects.
